title: The Right Time
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12-17-23 -PM- The Right Time - Galations 3_21-4_7 - Pastor Tito Lyro - 12_17_23, 1.58 PM.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Birth of Jesus in the Context of the Incarnation
subpoints: We worship not baby Jesus but Jesus as a complete person — God the Son
Christmas celebrates the incarnation of God's Son for a particular mission
Birth, life, death, and resurrection cannot be separated
Christianity is not a religion of stable and straw, but of thorns and nails, wood, and blood

outline.title: The Fullness of Time: God Orchestrated All of History for This Moment
subpoints: "When the fullness of the time had come, God sent forth His Son" (Galatians 4:4)
Scholars suggest historical reasons: Pax Romana, Greek language, synagogues everywhere
Ultimately, the time was right because God determined it — God does not get time wrong
Every breath, decision, and thought in all of history was orchestrated to arrive at Christmas

outline.title: Christ's Redeeming Mission: Born of a Woman, Born under the Law
subpoints: God is the initiator of both history and redemption
The Son existed before the incarnation — He was sent, which implies prior existence
"Born of a woman": the most primordial promise of redemption fulfilled (Genesis 3:15)
"Born under the law": the One who gave the law voluntarily placed Himself under it
Christ took the curse of the law upon Himself (Galatians 3:10)
Fully God and fully man united inseparably at the moment of conception in Mary's womb

outline.title: The Mission: Redemption and Adoption as Sons
subpoints: Jesus came to redeem — to purchase and deliver those enslaved by the debt of broken law
Christ redeems us primarily for adoption, not merely to release us from slavery
In the first century, only men were adopted; Paul's inclusion of women as "sons" (heirs) was revolutionary
Both men and women are heirs of the covenant promises of God
The proof of adoption: the Spirit crying "Abba, Father" in our hearts

outline.title: Conclusion: Sons and Heirs of the Promise
subpoints: Only Christians relate to God as Father — crawling into His lap
As sons, we are heirs of the promises made to Abraham: God is our God and we are His people
This identity unifies us across ethnicity, social status, and gender
God prepared and directed all of history so that at the perfect time He could send His Son to adopt us

summary: In this Advent evening sermon on Galatians 3:21-4:7, Pastor Lyro presents the birth of Jesus not as an isolated sentimental event but as a moment in the execution of an eternal, Trinitarian plan. Christmas celebrates the incarnation of God the Son, which cannot be separated from His life of obedience, His death on the cross, or His glorious resurrection. The timing was perfect — not because of historical circumstances alone, but because God determined it, having orchestrated every event in human history to arrive at exactly the right moment. Christ's mission is set out in two parallel clauses: He was born of a woman (fulfilling the primordial promise of Genesis 3:15 and demonstrating His full humanity) and born under the law (voluntarily subjecting Himself to the very law He gave, in order to take its curse and redeem those enslaved by it). But redemption was not the end goal — adoption was. Paul's declaration that both men and women are adopted as "sons" and therefore heirs would have been shocking to first-century Roman culture, where adoption was a legal transaction for male heirs only. Yet God in Christ has made every believer — male and female — a full heir of all that He has and all that He has promised. The proof of this adoption is the Holy Spirit crying "Abba, Father" in our hearts. The sermon concludes by noting that this adoption is the unifying identity of the people of God, transcending ethnicity, social status, and gender, and that it is the magnificent result of God having planned and worked all of history to arrive at the fullness of time.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Birth of Jesus in the Context of the Incarnation

We're going to be reading this afternoon from Galatians chapter 3, verse 21 to chapter 4 verse 7, as we continue our Advent series. When you've been pastoring for a while, it's hard to come up with new messages for Christmas. So we're trying to find different texts that speak of Christ — a good exercise for us to consider both the Old and New Testaments, instead of just Luke chapter 2 or Matthew chapter 1.

Galatians 3, starting at verse 21 through chapter 4 verse 7:

"Is the law then against the promises of God? Certainly not! For if there had been a law given which could have given life, truly righteousness would have been by the law. But the Scripture has confined all under sin, that the promise by faith in Jesus Christ might be given to those who believe. But before faith came, we were kept under guard by the law, kept for the faith which would afterward be revealed. Therefore the law was our tutor to bring us to Christ, that we might be justified by faith. But after faith has come, we are no longer under a tutor. For you are all sons of God through faith in Christ Jesus. For as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ. There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus. And if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise.

"Now I say that the heir, as long as he is a child, does not differ at all from a slave, though he is master of all, but is under guardians and stewards until the time appointed by the father. Even so we, when we were children, were in bondage under the elements of the world. But when the fullness of the time had come, God sent forth His Son, born of a woman, born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law, that we might receive the adoption as sons. And because you are sons, God has sent forth the Spirit of His Son into your hearts, crying out, 'Abba, Father!' Therefore you are no longer a slave but a son, and if a son, then an heir of God through Christ."

When we think about the birth of Jesus, it is important that we think of it in the context of the incarnation of God the Son — that is, God the Son becoming fully human in order to accomplish a mission. The point of celebrating Christmas is celebrating the incarnation of God's Son who came for a particular mission: perfect obedience, death on the cross, and resurrection for the salvation of His people.

The birth of Jesus cannot be separated from the life He lived. And His life cannot be separated from His death on the cross. And His death on the cross cannot be separated from His glorious resurrection from the dead. As Philip Ryken once said in response to someone who suggested replacing the cross as Christianity's symbol with the manger: Christianity is not a religion of stable and straw. It is a religion of thorns and nails, wood, and blood.

## The Fullness of Time: God Orchestrated All of History

Paul, under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, brings birth, life, death, and resurrection all together as he speaks of the mission of Jesus Christ. And in verse 4, he talks about the fullness of time: "But when the fullness of the time had come, God sent forth His Son."

The fullness of time — when time was just right, the ideal time. Scholars have suggested all kinds of different reasons why the time of Jesus's coming was the ideal time: the Pax Romana, the universal language of Greek, the widespread number of synagogues where the Gospel could be proclaimed. But ultimately, the time was right because God determined it was the right time. God doesn't get time wrong.

One thing we can say for sure is that God planned and worked out history so that Christ was born at exactly the best time to accomplish all the purposes the Father had for Him. Can you imagine that? Everything — every breath anyone has ever taken in history to that point, every decision, every travel, every thought — was orchestrated by God so that on that first Christmas, Christ was born. The whole purpose of all of creation, all of history, was to arrive at that moment.

## Christ's Redeeming Mission: Born of a Woman, Born under the Law

Christ's redeeming mission starts with God: "God sent His Son." He is the initiator of both history and redemption. Both start with God. God had already mentioned the covenant He made with Abraham in chapter 3, and in that covenant God said He would be the God of His people. God pursued His people even when they chose slavery instead of freedom. He relentlessly worked out history so that when the time was just right, He would send His Son to redeem His people — both Jew and Gentile — from bondage to sin, Satan, and self.

There is something more in the statement that "God sent His Son." His Son existed before the incarnation. The Son did not become the Son at the incarnation. To be sent, He already existed prior to that time. The Son was not an angel or a special creation — He was God Himself, sent with all the authority God has.

Paul continues by saying He was "born of a woman." The most primordial promise of redemption was that God was going to work through the seed of the woman — Genesis 3:15. Paul is tying that back in. In the midst of pronouncing a curse of death upon humanity because of the sin of its representative Adam, God promised life and victory over sin and Satan. Even in Genesis 3, when God is pronouncing consequences, He didn't leave humanity at a loss — He promised that the Savior would come.

While being "sent forth by God" shows that Jesus Christ was fully God, being "born of a woman" shows that Jesus Christ is fully human as well. At the conception of Jesus in Mary's womb by the Holy Spirit, these two natures were forever and inseparably united in one person.

Paul continues: He was "born under the law." The One who gave the law voluntarily placed Himself under the law, so that those who were under the law could be redeemed from the law. Christ put Himself under the law so that you and I could be freed from the condemnation of the law. James tells us that if you break one part of the law, you've broken the whole law. And yet Jesus takes the curse of the law upon Himself, as chapter 3 verse 10 tells us.

## The Mission: Redemption and Adoption as Sons

And why did He come? Verse 5 gives us the mission: "to redeem those who were under the law, that we might receive the adoption as sons." Jesus's mission was to redeem — to purchase, to deliver — those who had become slaves because of their debt to God through broken law.

But Christ doesn't redeem His people simply so that they can enter into a state of slavery to God. Though there are elements of that relationship, Christ redeems us from the bondage of the law, the bondage of Satan, and the bondage of self, primarily for us to be adopted as sons and daughters of God.

Now, the major modern translations all agree: "sons," not "sons and daughters" or "children." Because Paul is not using a collective masculine here — he is referring to a particular legal transaction. In the first century, women were never adopted. The idea of adoption was that the father would adopt a son or a capable slave to receive all the wealth of the family and carry on the family name. That is the adoption Paul is referring to.

So for Paul to say this and to include — as he does in chapter 3 verse 28-29 — male and female in this group as "sons," it would be something that a first-century citizen of the Roman Empire would hear and say: "This is crazy. This doesn't happen." Yet Paul says: we have all been adopted as sons, as those who receive the full inheritance of the Father in Christ — both men and women are heirs of the covenant promises of God. Unheard of in the culture.

The proof that this is true is the Spirit in us. Verse 6: "And because you are sons, God has sent forth the Spirit of His Son into your hearts, crying out, 'Abba, Father!'" The proof and guarantee that through Christ we are heirs of God is the Spirit who dwells in us and confirms that we have been adopted by God.

It is a distinctly Christian feature to relate to God as Father. Only Christians relate to their God as Father. As one who has been redeemed by the blood of Christ, we don't approach God as a judge or even just as the Creator — we crawl into His lap and call Him our Father.

## Conclusion: Sons and Heirs of the Promise

And as sons and daughters of God, we are heirs of the promises of God — especially the promises made to Abraham, which boil down to God being our God and our being His people. This promise echoes throughout the entire Bible. And that is really what the promise is: for all eternity, the all-glorious God is going to be our God and we are going to be His people.

This identity unifies us. It's not ethnicity that unifies us — Jews and Gentiles are together. It's not social status — slave and free are in this group. It's not gender — male and female are in this group. What identifies us is that God is our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ is our Savior. It is faith in Christ that binds the people of God — because it welcomes us into one family.

So God, who is a God of love, prepared and directed history so that at the right time — the perfect time — He could send forth His Son to redeem His people. And that is you and I who are adopted sons of God, heirs of all, and heirs of the promises of the covenant that God is our God and we are His people.

Father in heaven, we thank You that You are God. We pray that we would be satisfied with that and content with You. We thank You that You have adopted us as Your sons — legal heirs of all that You have. We pray that we look to that time and that place when we will see our Savior as He is, for we shall be like Him. We pray that You write these words in the tablets of our hearts. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
